blacksmith_start.text:
You are born in a poor village in central Italy.
You are the second son, the fourth child.
The poor land of your father cannot support all of you.

blacksmith_100.text:
Your parents discussed.
Staying is not an option for you.
Ostia is where you will be sent.
Not quite an opportunity.
You did not know what you would be facing.
You can’t blame your family.
The extra mouth is a burden.
You kept your tears.
You farewell your family.
You step on your own journey at the age of 11.

blacksmith_101.text:
It feels like humiliation.
You carry charcoal.
You work the bellows.
You scoop water.
All these dirty works.
You obey, obey, and obey.

blacksmith_103.text:
You are blessed.
Your master values your diligence.
Luckily, you are smart enough.
You learn to hold tongs.
You read heat by color.
You have a chance to craft small things.
A lock, a tool, a door hinge.
You enjoyed this.

blacksmith_104.text:
You worship Vulcan.
You joined Vulcanalia.
You learned to pray.
He will bless you.

blacksmith_105.text:
You are trusted more.
You realized.
Any mistakes, misses, or wrong timing cost your master.
You are talented.
You never miss.

blacksmith_106.text:
Trajan’s victory brought opportunities.
They want ironworks.
They want too much, so you see a chance.
Over the years, you learned skills.
How to craft, how to repair, how to adjust.
You earned freedom with your savings.
Your master looked at you with pride.
But also with some worry.
You established your own blacksmith.
For the first time, your labor brings gold to your own pocket.

blacksmith_109.text:
You expanded your shop.
You would do anything.
You fit wagons, make tools, craft swords.
You wouldn’t know how much blood your sword will touch.
You wouldn’t care.
You didn’t know how your family was.
You didn’t know whether to hate them or appreciate them.

blacksmith_112.text:
You embraced your first apprentice.
You see yourself in his immature face.
You were stunned for a second.
When he was sent by his family.
A different feeling emerges inside you.
A little bit bitter, a little bit sad.
You decide to treat him:

choices.label: Like how your master treats you

blacksmith_master_path_114.text:
He learned quickly, even better than you.
He learned how to read color.
He learned how to finish a blade.
You still sent labor work, the dirty one, to him.
You see him sweating, suffering, but never complaining.
You felt a little uneasy.
You shook away that thought.
You kept working on the immersive orders.

blacksmith_master_path_121.text:
He left, as you did when you were young.
He opened a shop right across from you.
You didn’t worry, because half of his shop belongs to you, too.
You did less and less work.
No more blades, no more wagons, just some casual things only.

blacksmith_master_path_129.text:
You felt the furnace smoke in your lungs.
It burns.
You can no longer lift, you can no longer craft.
You felt death near you.

blacksmith_master_path_130.text:
Your student buries you.
You have no family, no children, no one.
He puts a hammer next to you.
He blesses you with the honor of Vulcan.

blacksmith_son_path_114.text:
You gave him some dirty work, but not that much.
You didn’t use him as your master used you.
You taught him skills.
How to read fire.
How to read timing.
How to craft pieces.
You sometimes see a smile on his face.
That’s what was not on you.

blacksmith_son_path_116.text:
He learned quickly, really quickly.
He has even more talent than you do.
He crafts better than you.
Faster, neater, stronger.
You gave your trust in him.

blacksmith_son_path_120.text:
You work less and less.
He stayed at your shop.
He works with passion.
He enjoyed crafting.

blacksmith_son_path_121.text:
You stopped crafting.
You work with numbers.
You are backing up.

blacksmith_son_path_125.text:
Illness and furnace smoke didn’t catch you like other experienced masters.
You sometimes still wander in your store, but you no longer work.
He worked well, really well.
You see him taking apprentices and treating them well.
You felt accomplished.
It’s like seeing your son having a family.
You felt pride.

blacksmith_son_path_130.text:
With the blessing of Vulcan, you closed your eyes.
He hosts your burial.
With respect, with gratitude, and with love.
He put aside your hammer.
Your name stays with your store forever.

builder_start.text:
You are born in a small house on the edge of the city.
Your father is a farmer.
The grain of his work only allows your family not to starve.

builder_95.text:
You show strength as a boy.
You help your father in the field.
Carrying water, plowing dirt, planting seeds.
You also help your uncle build things.
Carrying stones, concrete, and heavy weights.

builder_100.text:
Your uncle once needed your help building a room for school.
You look at the children learning letters by teachers.
You couldn’t understand the signs.
You couldn’t understand words.
These seem privileges to you.
What you learn is how to lift, how to build, taught by your uncle.

builder_103.text:
You learned some techniques.
Mostly about materials, building balance, or fitting things in.
Those small skills make you worth slightly more.
Still, you are paid little, but what can you do?

builder_111.text:
You worked in many buildings.
But those don’t belong to you.
They pay you just enough to live, but sometimes you starve too.
You could never earn enough to stop working.
Because that’s how Rome works.

builder_116.text:
You enjoyed drinking.
You find it fun in thermopolia.
You couldn’t join the table of small gamble.
Because you know the money is for your stomach.

builder_124.text:
Your body starts to tell you to stop.
Your shoulders ache, and you move slow.
You are not that old.
But age starts killing you.

builder_137.text:
You meet a younger laborer.
He follows you for work.
You showed him how to lift with less effort.
You showed him how to align for good.
But for yourself, you sense something bad.

builder_138.text:
You are too old to lift.
You are too old to work.
You are too old to live.
The last night you spent was in thermopolia.
You get an extra drink.
You sleep on the road.

fighter_start.text:
You are born into a family in a small tribe.
Your mother luckily did not die during childbirth.
Your father, looking at you as a boy, laughed from the bottom of his heart.
You heard him talking to people around him.
You don’t understand what he was saying, but you can see the pride in his eyes.

fighter_80.text:
You can barely talk; the old man in the tribe taught you words.
You enjoyed it.
One day, you run into your father’s meeting.
You see his face frowning, and you hear some words.
You didn’t fully understand, but you see the anger in his eyes.

fighter_76.text:
Your tribe was invaded.
Your father, teacher, cousins, and most people you know have died.
You see them pierced by arrows, or chopped by swords, all covered in blood.
You see your mother lying on the ground, facing down.
You nudged her arm, trying to wake her up.
You see something shiny on your left.
You lift your head up, and you see a Roman soldier.
He pointed his spear at you, and you looked him in the eye.
He put his shield down and picked you up by your neck.
You didn’t cry.
You were brought to the Roman troops.

fighter_75.text:
Your year in the Roman army wasn’t easy.
You belong to calones.
You need to carry wood for their needs and do all the dirty work.
They yell, they whip, and you see a traitor, your “friend” in calones, died midway through his escape.

fighter_74.text:
They brought you back to Rome.
It is a huge city, and you see houses twice as big as the one you lived in.
You are auctioned.
You stand on the stage, naked.
The merchants examine you, one by one.
You felt their touches, and you closed your eyes.
You felt stabbing pain in your neck.
You opened your eyes, and you are picked.
The merchant fancied your muscles.
He handed you a piece of bread and told you to be a gladiator.
You listened to him bragging, while wolfing down the piece of bread, you needed it.
You are sent to the gladiatorial school.

fighter_73_choice.text:
Capua.

You see many men here.
Gauls, Thracians, and many from different places.
Some are fierce, some are violent, some only want blood.
You began training.
A wooden sword and a crappy shield are all you have.
You were undefeated.
Of course you were, or otherwise you would not be alive.
You meet friends, true friends, the ones you trained with.
But you also know, at some time, only one of you will die.
Among your friends, one is named Spartacus.
He whispered:
“Do you hate?”

fighter_rebellion_73.text:
Your plots are discovered.
There's no more time.
You all grabbed whatever you could.
Kitchen knives, spits, sticks.
You killed your way out.
You all climbed to Versius.
You are all surrounded by Roman troops.
They tried to trap you all with hunger, thirst, and fear.
You descend a wild vine rope, sending warriors down to the Roman troops at night.
You all destroyed the camp.
You see one of the captive familiar.
Memory stroke you 10 years ago.
He was the one pushing a dagger into your mom’s heart.
He begged you for mercy.
“I’m not Roman, I’m forced to be in the army.”
You grasped your dagger, and your arm started shaking.
You cut his throat.

fighter_rebellion_72.text:
The leaders do not all agree.
You followed Spartacus for the passion in his eyes.
Crixus, a dear leader in the rebellion, is killed.
You honor him, but you see your future on his dead body.

fighter_rebellion_71_choice.text:
Marcus Licinius Crassus took over the Roman troops.
You felt the war with them tighter, harder, and colder.
The final battle comes.
Before it began, Spartacus killed his horse.
If you all win, he shall gain a better one.
If you all lose, he will need no one.
No one is leaving this field alive.
You clenched your dagger, the one accompanied you since the start.
You rush toward the Romans with your friends.

fighter_legend_ending.text:
You fight till the end.
Your body is covered with blood.
It was an honor.
You are not a failure.

fighter_coward_ending.text:
You feared.
You dropped your armor.
You kneeled to Rome.
They nailed you on the cross.
The traitors were shown to the public on the street.
The cowards were humiliated.

fighter_school_72.text:
You stay inside the wall.
You fear danger, though now you are still facing it.
Soon after, you are sold.
New yard, new trainer, same life.

fighter_school_72_training.text:
Wooden sword, broken shield.
Again and again.
You grow heavy and strong.
You kill more and more.
People applaud for the blood on your sword.

fighter_school_71.text:
Your trainer shared the bad news.
Spartacus died with his brothers.
You mourn for him; the school didn’t.
You restored.
Picking up the sword and shield.
Rome fears the rebels, but the game still goes on.

fighter_school_70.text:
You are no legend.
Winning gives you nothing.
Besides scars and wounds.
You saw friends never getting up.
You see newcomers thrown in.

fighter_school_67.text:
The wounds are a burden.
They hurt, but the fear of death hurts more.
All the faces in blood appeared at night.
The next one could be your last one.

fighter_school_66.text:
You stand in the amphitheater.
A man standing on the opposite side.
He is younger, has no injuries, and is stronger.
You barely blocked his swing.
Your attack is no longer sharpened.
He knocked off your shield.
You see his blade piercing through your chest.
You fell.
The crowd curses.
The crowd yells.
You closed your eyes.

trader_start.text:
You are born in a town south of Rome.
You will not starve, but there isn’t spare in your family.

trader_100.text:
You start working as a boy.
You carry sacks.
You load carts.
You earn what you want.
As your father cannot give them to you.

trader_96.text:
Your father taught you how to accumulate wealth.
You learned how to contain your desire.
You learn how wealth works.
The more you accumulate, the more you can play with it.

trader_92.text:
You started your “business.”
You buy produce from the cheap.
You carry it to where it sells better.
Oil, fish, cloth, tools.
You are not yet a great merchant.
You earn little or nothing most of the time.
Your father supports you, not financially, but mentally.

trader_88.text:
War and unease threaten your trade.
Roads are less safe.
Prices are unstable.
A bad decision costs you months of gain.
You learn risks.
You learn profit.

trader_84.text:
You become more cautious.
You suppress your ambition.
You distinguish between gambling and trading.

trader_80.text:
You have enough to run an actual business.
You hire transport.
You bargain with scale.
You deal with people directly.
You are part of the business.
Your decision matters.

trader_76.text:
You start actions in Rome.
The city is the challenge.
The scale scares you.
What was big for the town can’t even be seen by Rome.

trader_75.text:
You learned more about business.
In Rome, reputation is all.
Relationships and trust didn’t come from nowhere.
You sometimes get nowhere.
But you are patient enough.

trader_72.text:
You stand firm in the city.
You understand what is essential in Rome.
Food, transport, supply.
Those make you not the wealthiest, but with a steady life.

trader_68_choice.text:
You accumulated wealth.
You grew in scale.
You find politics in markets.
You learned quickly that merchants are not in charge.
People who control laws, taxes, and the city can ruin you quickly.
From here, you decide:

trader_politics_67.text:
You build relationships.
With a politician, with aristocrats, with the higher class.
You gift them.
You flatter them.
You place yourself lower.
You tolerate.

trader_politics_64.text:
You know Catiline.
You learned his plan.
You decided.
This is the time you claim power.

trader_politics_63.text:
You failed.
The shine of power blinded your father’s words.
You urged too much.
Too much so that it backfires on you.
You lost everything.

trader_quit_67.text:
You’ve accumulated enough.
Enough wealth to free you from working for the rest of your life.
You aren’t eager for power.
You stepped away from politics, as you see the dangers in it.

trader_quit_66.text:
You returned to your town.
You bought land.
A lot of land.
You lend them.
You earn from them.

trader_quit_63.text:
Living in a huge house, you heard what happened in the city.
You felt lucky for leaving politics.
You enjoyed your life.
You spent time with your family.
Inside your town,
You are the success.

poet_origin_choice.text:
Fortuna does not only decide what you become.
She also decides where you begin.

Will you be born poor, with only your voice and memory to carry you?
Or will you be born rich, protected by family, education, and name?

poet_poor_start.text:
You are born in a suburb of Rome.
Your family lives by labor.
Your father works in the sun, and your mother earns some small earnings.

poet_poor_98.text:
You grew with love.
Your mother values your education.
She taught you enough letters.
You read, you memorize, you recite.
Your education is not as polished as that of the wealthy.
But you're smart, you work hard, and you enjoy.

poet_poor_93.text:
God blessed you with a good memory and a beautiful voice.
People know you in town, and people recognize you by voice.
You sing to your mother, and she praises.
You sing to your father, but he’s too tired to enjoy.
Your blessed voice took away your talent in body.
You are skinny, and you barely work.
You see your father frowning sometimes.
And you understand what it means.

poet_poor_90.text:
A wealthier family in your town invited you.
They are meeting with a famous merchant from the city.
You joined their dinner.
But you stand aside.
You sing to entertain, which is the reason you’re invited.
You see ignorance in their eyes.
Both the host and the guest.
You kept singing.
Because this is your first earning.
Because you want to make your father proud.
Because you know your voice is why you matter.

poet_poor_86.text:
You left your town.
You brought your savings.
Little, but enough.
Your destiny is the city.
You wish to earn fame.
You wish to be recognized.
You wish to be remembered.

poet_poor_84.text:
You started to write some short lines on your own.
You still sing, and you still recite.
People are more generous in the city.
You wish to live by your pen more than by your mouth.
You wander from place to place and hope one day you can settle down.
You are sometimes invited to a rich house.
By there you sell your voice.
You hate to see their face.
They show no respect.

poet_poor_82.text:
Sulla’s victory fills Rome with fear.
You see people dying on the streets and in houses.
Survival is silence.
The city taught you.
Politics destroys lives faster than hunger.

poet_poor_75.text:
Your savings ran out.
Livelihood forces you to wander on the streets.
You sing.
Drinking song.
Flattering verse.
You hate these.
You admire Odyssey.
You admire the Iliad.
You wish to write epics.
But your fate seems destined since your birth.

poet_poor_63.text:
The city is filled with tension and rumor.
Cicero with his ambition.
People argue over Catiline.
They are shaping history.

poet_poor_62.text:
You squeeze into the crowd.
Cicero speaks for Archias.
You hear enough.
You know enough.
You understand enough.
Rome praises poets.
But the poets who win respect are those who stand by a patron.
Poetry is art.
But poetry in Rome is already polluted with politics.

poet_poor_59.text:
The winter is deadly to you.
No more hosts, no more guests.
Cold weakens you.
You closed your eyes.
You are too tired of Rome.

poet_rich_start.text:
You were born in Rome.
Your crying echoes in your huge house.
A servant took over you.
He showed you to your father.
His face glows with happiness.

poet_rich_98.text:
You went to a private school.
You like your grammaticus and rhetor.
They told your parents about your talent.
“A gift by god,”
they said.
You read aloud well.
You learned to admire style.
You speak with confidence.
You are eloquent.

poet_rich_93.text:
You recite in front of your father.
His eyes shine with praise.
You recite in front of your teachers.
Their eyes shine with pride.
You recite in front of the guests.
They praised.
But you didn’t know for your poem or for your father.

poet_rich_86.text:
The republic is shaken by war.
Your family is your best protection.
You devote yourself to literature.
No politics, no dangers.

poet_rich_84.text:
You are brought to connections.
You are in a circle where educated men admire literature like you.
You speak about literature.
You enjoy discussions.
Those who are admired by scholars,
like Archias, become people you get in touch with.
You are not a performer.
Neither are you someone who lives off his work.
You are a poet.

poet_rich_77.text:
You become a master of poetry.
You discuss them seriously.
You design verse.
You start shaping your work.
You start building your fame.
You start to find your worth.

poet_rich_68.text:
The society taught you something more about poetry.
It’s not only a form of art.
It’s not only an expression of feeling.
It’s a tool, a weapon, something powerful enough.
You see its connection with politics.

poet_rich_62.text:
You attend when Cicero defends Archias.
Not just a listener.
You held a place among audiences.
You learned more about poetry.
Pro Archia is not only a speech.
It connects literature with power.
You remembered it as your tool.

poet_rich_59.text:
Catullus brought a new fashion.
Quicker, sharper, more polished.
You admire his style.
Still, you remained in the classic way.

poet_rich_56.text:
You have seen enough of how fame works.
You pursue literature still.
Your family savings are enough for the rest of your life.
You are respected in your poetry group.
Some of your verses are copied.
Some of your verses are recited.
Your name is remembered.

poet_rich_49.text:
Caesar started the civil war.
The tension in the city tired you.
You’ve seen enough.
You’ve heard enough.
You’ve been through enough.
Illness caught you with your old age.
Your family gave you a decent burial.
Some of your works are preserved.
You are respected, but are you famous?

soldier_start.text:
You were born in northern Italy.
Your family has warmth.
You don’t have to starve.
But you can’t expect more.

soldier_104.text:
You volunteer for the army.
You felt honored to fight for Trajan.
You are strong enough.
They promise you awards.
But you know that will be far away.

soldier_105.text:
The training is hard.
It’s also boring and repetitive.
Physical training.
Weapon training.
You keep formation, build camp, and most importantly, obey orders.
Order is everything.
You sleep in tents, eat by rations, and share burdens.

soldier_106.text:
You joined the war against Dacia.
It is your first time on the battlefield.
You are excited, but a little scared.
But Rome doesn’t allow cowardice.
You didn’t fight like hero.
You hold formation, obey orders, and trust men beside you.
Victory came, less heroic, less exciting.

soldier_107.text:
The victory didn’t bring you much honor and awards.
Those went to emperors and generals.
You gained pretty much nothing.
But you see your peers with scars, limps, and wounds.
You didn’t feel like you were part of it, even though you were.

soldier_111.text:
You become a minor leader.
Still, you are under many layers of commands.
You obey orders and teach newbies to obey orders.
You are not a great man.
The real great man is above you.
All you do is listen to his order.

soldier_123.text:
You receive an honorable discharge.
They recognize you as a veteran.
Kids ask you about fights.
You would share with them how you all conquered.
But do you actually enjoy those?

soldier_124.text:
You settle in a town.
The money is not enough to keep you healthy, but it is enough to support your life.
Some lands.
A house.
More tranquil in your life.

soldier_136.text:
You suffered from wounds in war.
They hurt you badly at night.
One day, you are tired.
Tired from scenes flashing at night.
Tired of living your life.
You closed your eyes.
Your burial was respected and honored.
